# Questions in Livestream 173

Live Stream #173: I never said she stole my money

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=495" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:15</a> | Why are Judges 1:10-15 and Joshua 15:13-19 almost word for word? | He treats Judges as continuing the Joshua narrative, so the repeated episode is not surprising. | He explains that Judges opens by recapping material from Joshua and turning it into a prologue for the judges period. The repetition marks literary continuity, not a separate contradiction, because Judges 1 resumes where Joshua left off and uses the older conquest account to orient the next narrative section. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=863" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:23</a> | Does Deuteronomy 32:8 mean El gave Yahweh Israel, making El the father of Yahweh? | He rejects that reading and stresses the passage's textual complexity without separating Yahweh from the Most High that way. | He walks through the textual variants and argues that the passage does not require two separate gods, one assigning Israel to another. His answer keeps Yahweh identified with the Most High and treats Israel's allotment as part of a complex divine-council text rather than evidence that Yahweh was a subordinate son of El. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=1417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:37</a> | Have you ever been an Exodus mythicist? | No. He says the closest he came was simple indifference or agnosticism about the question. | He says he was never committed to denying the Exodus; at most, earlier in his life he had not investigated it closely and did not care much about the question. He contrasts that lack of interest with actual mythicism and says further study moved him away from indifference. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=1530" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:30</a> | Doesn't the reference to Pithom in Exodus 1:11 peg the event to Ramesses II? | Yes. He says the site's fortification and repopulation line up with Ramesses II's reign. | He treats Pithom as evidence for a Ramesside setting because the site is associated with fortification and renewed occupation under Ramesses II. The reference does not by itself answer every Exodus chronology question, but for him it strongly fits the thirteenth-century context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=1679" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:59</a> | Is the serpent in Genesis 3 a snake? | No. He identifies the serpent as a spiritual being, using serpent language metaphorically rather than describing an ordinary reptile. | He says the Hebrew term can refer broadly to serpent-like creatures, but Genesis 3 is not mainly zoological. In his reading the figure is a spiritual rebel described with serpent imagery, so the text is using creature language to characterize a personal spiritual being. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=1787" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:47</a> | When can biblical time periods, dates, days, and year counts start being taken literally? | He says literal dates can still be hard to reconstruct because calendars changed, and many early year counts remain approximate or idiomatic. | He cautions that ancient chronological notices cannot be handled by a single modern rule because calendars, regnal accounting, and idiomatic number use vary. Even when later dates become more anchored, conversion into modern years can remain complicated and some early figures should be treated with caution. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=2238" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:18</a> | How did Adam and Eve realize they were naked if clothes were not a thing yet, and why were they ashamed? | He says nakedness symbolizes exposed sin and shame; the issue is not clothing itself but trying to cover sin. | He frames the scene as theological symbolism rather than a practical discovery of clothing. Adam and Eve's shame comes from guilt and exposure after sin, and the covering motif points to humans attempting to hide or manage that guilt before God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=2408" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:08</a> | How should God walking in the garden in Genesis 3:8 be explained to a child? | He says the text shows God present in sacred space, drawing Adam and Eve out of hiding after their sin. | He suggests explaining that God is present with them in the garden, which functions like sacred space, and that their hiding shows the broken relationship caused by sin. The point for a child is not to over-define God's body but to see God coming to confront and restore. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=2715" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:15</a> | Why haven't you written a Sunday School curriculum? | He says he once proposed one, but the church wanted lighter material rather than a serious educational curriculum. | He recounts having offered a more substantial curriculum idea, but says the response was that it was too serious for what the church wanted. His answer implies that the problem was not unwillingness to teach children, but a mismatch between his educational goals and institutional expectations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=3021" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:21</a> | Is there an Enoch-like figure at number seven in Egyptian king or god lists? | No. He says Egyptian king-and-god lists do not preserve a direct parallel of that kind. | He says he is not aware of an Egyptian list where the seventh figure functions like Enoch in Genesis. Egyptian king and god lists have their own structures, but he does not see the specific seventh-position motif needed for the proposed comparison. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=3255" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:15</a> | Why does the Old Testament forbid eating leavened bread during Passover? | He says yeast is associated with mold and decay, so removing leaven fits Passover purity and cleansing rather than a blanket rejection of yeast. | He connects the command to symbolic removal of corruption in a feast context. Leaven is not treated as intrinsically evil in all settings, but at Passover its removal communicates cleansing, separation from decay, and ritual readiness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=3761" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:41</a> | Why did John choose conquest, war, famine, and death as riders in Revelation? | He connects the four horsemen to Zechariah 6 and says John is describing agents of divine wrath and judgment. | He reads Revelation's riders against Old Testament prophetic imagery, especially Zechariah's horses. The sequence of conquest, war, famine, and death represents covenant-judgment disasters released under divine authority rather than arbitrary apocalyptic decoration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=4065" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:45</a> | Is it really true that we do not know how the pyramids were built? | He says we know the broad method fairly well from quarries, transport evidence, unfinished monuments, and construction remains. | He rejects the popular claim that pyramid construction is a mystery in the strong sense. While details remain debated, evidence from quarrying, hauling, unfinished structures, worker installations, and construction traces gives a reasonably clear picture of the broad process. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=4196" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:56</a> | Did building pyramids have an economic impact, and is there documentation for that? | He says yes, citing traditions such as Papyrus Westcar that remember major pyramid builders as economically burdensome rulers. | He says large royal building programs would have required major labor and material organization, so economic effects are expected. He points to later Egyptian traditions remembering pyramid-building kings as burdensome, while treating such sources as suggestive rather than modern budget records. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=4380" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:00</a> | Why do people use the pyramids as an argument against trusting the Bible or believing in God? | He says that argument misunderstands both Egyptian history and the Bible, since the Bible does not claim Israelites built the pyramids. | He argues that skeptics often assume a false claim, namely that the Bible says Israel built the pyramids. Since the biblical text does not say that, pyramid chronology is not an argument against Scripture; it is mostly a misunderstanding repeated in popular debate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=4606" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:46</a> | Does Mark 4:35-40 connect to Psalm 3 or Psalm 4:8? | He says either psalm could be applied secondarily, but Mark does not make an explicit quotation or direct connection. | He allows that readers can see thematic resonance with sleeping in safety or divine deliverance, but he does not see Mark signaling a formal citation. The connection may be devotional or typological, not an exegetically required link. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=4802" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:02</a> | Are there any non-Christian sources for Herod's massacre, or even general outside evidence? | He says there is no direct outside source and that ancient historians may have regarded it as too minor to record. | He notes that Herod's cruelty is well known generally, but the Bethlehem massacre itself is not independently attested outside Matthew. He argues that a small local killing of infants may not have appeared significant enough for ancient historians to preserve, especially amid Herod's larger crimes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=4935" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:15</a> | Does Deuteronomy 3:4-5 fit a late thirteenth-century BC conquest if many Canaanite cities were unwalled? | He says yes, because the text itself distinguishes fortified cities from many unwalled towns. | He points out that Deuteronomy does not claim every settlement was a walled city. The passage distinguishes fortified places from numerous unwalled towns, which leaves room for archaeological evidence that many Late Bronze settlements lacked walls. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=5392" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:52</a> | Have you read Paul and the Resurrection of Israel? | No. He says he does not usually comment on books he has not read. | He declines to evaluate the book because he has not read it. The answer is limited intentionally: he avoids giving a secondhand assessment and says his usual practice is not to comment on works he has not personally engaged. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=5409" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:09</a> | What do you wish more people knew about the biblical feasts? | He highlights that the ban on boiling a kid in its mother's milk is a feast-related prohibition against a pagan ritual, not a general cheeseburger ban. | He uses the feast laws to correct a common overreading of the milk-and-meat prohibition. In his view the command targets a particular ritual practice associated with pagan worship, so it should be read in its covenantal feast context rather than turned into a universal dietary rule. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=5915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:35</a> | Can you give sources for reading on boiling a kid in its mother's milk? | He says he found it in a commentary and connects it to Ugaritic texts, but he cannot name the specific source from memory. | He remembers encountering the argument in commentary literature and links it to Ugaritic material, but he cannot provide the exact citation during the stream. The answer therefore supports the general direction while explicitly leaving the bibliography unresolved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=6084" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:24</a> | What is the best case that Deuteronomy resembles first-millennium treaties more than second-millennium treaties? | He says he does not think there is a strong case and would defend a late Bronze Age core for Deuteronomy on other grounds. | He says he is not persuaded that Deuteronomy is best explained by first-millennium treaty parallels. He would instead argue for an older core and separate later editorial activity from the underlying covenant material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=6186" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:06</a> | Have we elevated the law to be more than intended? | He says yes, if the law is being read as prescriptive modern law rather than ancient Near Eastern case law. | He explains that biblical law often functions like ancient case law, giving paradigms and wisdom for judges rather than a modern exhaustive statute book. Elevating it wrongly means forcing it into a legal category it was not designed to fill. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=6504" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:24</a> | Why did Jesus get baptized in Matthew 3:15? | He says Jesus' baptism fulfilled righteousness and marked him for priestly service, not repentance from sin. | He rejects the idea that Jesus needed baptism because of personal sin. Instead, he connects the event to fulfilling righteousness, public commissioning, and priestly identification at the beginning of Jesus' ministry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=6894" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:54</a> | Is it true that in the ancient Near East a year was 360 days? | He says it depends on the calendar; some civil calendars could be 360 days, while Egyptian, lunar, and lunisolar calendars differed. | He warns against flattening all ancient calendars into one model. Some systems used schematic 360-day years, Egypt added epagomenal days, and lunar or lunisolar calendars handled months and intercalation differently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=7070" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:50</a> | What should be said to people who claim John 6:51-58 is about the Eucharist? | He says the passage is primarily about Jesus as the living bread and manna fulfillment, not directly about the Eucharist. | He reads John 6 first through the bread-from-heaven and manna themes in the chapter. Eucharistic theology may draw on the passage later, but he says the immediate point is faith in Jesus as the living bread who gives life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=7494" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:54</a> | Why does the early church claim Joachim is Mary's father when the Bible says Eli? | He says that claim likely comes from the Protoevangelium of James, an apocryphal source he regards as unreliable. | He attributes Joachim to later apocryphal tradition rather than biblical data. Since he regards the Protoevangelium of James as historically unreliable, he does not treat the name as a secure correction to Luke's genealogy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=7688" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:08</a> | How is Old Arabic linguistically different from Quranic and modern Arabic? | He says he is not an Arabic specialist and can only speak generally about Old Arabic's Semitic and alphabetic features. | He limits his answer because Arabic historical linguistics is not his specialization. He can identify Old Arabic broadly as an earlier Semitic language form preserved in inscriptions and scripts, but he does not claim expertise in the detailed differences from Quranic or modern Arabic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=7791" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:51</a> | What does the Spirit do in baptism according to Scripture? | He says he does not have a firm view because the text is vague; he distinguishes regeneration and baptism as a pledge of new life. | He says Scripture does not spell out the Spirit's baptismal action with the precision some traditions want. He separates regeneration from the rite itself and describes baptism as a pledge or sign connected with new life, while leaving details tentative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=7993" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:13</a> | Does turning the other cheek mean not reacting to insults, or asking to be treated as an equal rather than a slave? | He says it means not repaying insult with evil, not a request for equal social treatment. | He interprets the teaching as a command against retaliating to insult and escalation. The point is not primarily a coded demand for social equality, but a call to absorb dishonor without answering evil in kind. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=8265" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:45</a> | Why do Christians keep making bad films? | He blames lazy writing and urges Christians in the arts to learn their craft and make excellent work. | He says the problem is often poor craft, shallow writing, and assuming a Christian message can compensate for weak art. His advice is that Christians who want to make films should learn storytelling, production, and discipline instead of excusing mediocrity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=9323" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:23</a> | As an evidentialist, how are you still a Christian? | He says he remains Christian because he is an evidentialist and thinks online claims that there is no evidence are ignorant. | He argues that the evidence question is exactly why he remains Christian, not a reason to abandon faith. He criticizes online claims that Christianity has no evidence as historically uninformed and says evidential evaluation supports his commitment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=9411" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:51</a> | What is the best documented case for a Eucharistic miracle? | He says he does not track Eucharistic miracle claims and prefers to ground his faith in Scripture rather than secondary miracle reports. | He does not offer a ranked case because he has not studied that category closely. He says his confidence does not rest on such miracle reports, and he prefers to ground belief in Scripture and central Christian claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=9566" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:26</a> | Are Luke 1:6 and Romans 10:13 contradictory? | He says Romans 10:13 does not seem connected; if the intended text is Romans 3:10, the passages use righteousness in different senses. | He first notes that Romans 10:13 does not appear to create the proposed tension. If the question meant Romans 3:10, he distinguishes covenantal or relative righteousness in Luke from Paul's broader statement about universal sinfulness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=10194" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:54</a> | How does transgression differ from abomination and sin? | He distinguishes abomination on a holiness/cleanness spectrum from sin categories such as iniquity, transgression, and rebellion. | He explains that abomination language often concerns ritual, holiness, or uncleanness, while sin terms can describe moral failure, rebellion, guilt, or crossing a boundary. The categories overlap but are not simple synonyms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=10552" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:52</a> | Were there gangs in ancient Egypt, and is there evidence of gang culture? | He says gangs could appear when civil order broke down, but he does not describe Egypt as having a persistent gang culture. | He allows that banditry or organized violence can emerge during periods of weak state control. However, he does not know evidence for a stable, modern-style gang culture in Egypt and frames such activity as episodic disorder. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=10640" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:20</a> | What do you think about reading the Genesis 5 genealogy as a prophecy of Jesus? | He rejects it as thoroughly debunked and says it relies on speculative name meanings and hidden-message exegesis. | He says the argument depends on uncertain etymologies and stringing name meanings into a hidden message. For him that is not sound exegesis, and he treats the viral prophecy reading as already discredited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=10841" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:41</a> | What are the main problems with Protestantism? | He declines the question as too broad and divisive, saying he would address specific theological issues instead. | He says the question is too sweeping to answer usefully in a live Q&A and would invite unnecessary conflict. He prefers concrete questions about particular doctrines, practices, or arguments rather than a generalized critique of Protestantism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=10978" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:58</a> | Do you think human beings existed before Adam and Eve? | He says the text may allow that possibility, with Adam and Eve serving as specially appointed representatives of humanity. | He says Genesis can be read in a way that does not require Adam and Eve to be the only humans alive if they are functioning as chosen priestly or representative figures. He presents that as a possible reading, not a settled dogmatic requirement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=11275" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:55</a> | What do Leviathan, Behemoth, Rahab, and similar figures in Job represent? | He identifies them as chaos monsters or spirit beings associated with creation and later replaced by the divine council's role. | He treats these beings as part of ancient chaos-monster imagery tied to creation, cosmic order, and spiritual powers. In later biblical theology, he says the divine council framework takes over much of the conceptual role these figures occupy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=11440" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:40</a> | Do people who are brain dead still have their soul? | He says he is not qualified to answer definitively; a bioethicist in the room says many would answer yes because they are not fully dead. | He explicitly limits his own authority on the bioethical question and turns to the specialist present. The answer given is that many would say yes, because brain death does not necessarily settle every question about whether the person is fully dead. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=11559" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:39</a> | If the Shroud is not Jesus' burial cloth, was it a painting of him? | He says the cloth does not date to Jesus and may be a painting, but he withholds fuller claims pending experimental work. | He says he is skeptical that the Shroud is Jesus' burial cloth and mentions the possibility of a painted or otherwise produced image. He does not press the claim beyond his evidence and says more detailed evaluation depends on experimental work he plans to discuss elsewhere. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=11678" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:38</a> | What role should tradition play in theology? | He says tradition can guide theology, but its value depends on whether it grows from exegesis or from later cultural assumptions. | He distinguishes between tradition as accumulated exegetical wisdom and tradition as inherited cultural habit. Tradition can be helpful and deserves attention, but it cannot override Scripture or be treated as equally reliable without scrutiny. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=11784" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:24</a> | Who is your favorite early church figure, and why? | He does not choose one favorite, but names figures such as Clement, Chrysostom, Ambrose, Irenaeus, and Theophilus of Antioch. | He says he has several figures he appreciates for different reasons rather than a single favorite. The names he gives span apologetic, pastoral, and exegetical interests, showing admiration for their contributions without ranking them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=11886" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:06</a> | Where was the Garden of Eden located? | He says we do not know for sure, but he would guess near Sealand, much of which is now underwater. | He says the location cannot be identified with confidence from the available data. If pressed for a guess, he looks toward the Sealand or southern Mesopotamian region, while noting that geography and water levels complicate any modern reconstruction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=12018" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:18</a> | What kind of knowledge of evil do divine beings or angels have? | He says holy angels have intellectual knowledge of evil, while fallen beings have experiential knowledge of evil. | He distinguishes knowing that evil exists from participating in evil. Holy beings can understand evil intellectually without committing it, while fallen spiritual beings know evil by experience because they rebelled. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=12194" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:14</a> | Is the Septuagint addition about Pharaoh conquering Gezer in Joshua 16 likely the Merneptah campaign? | No. He identifies it as a scribal gloss about Gezer connected with Solomon, not the Merneptah campaign. | He rejects linking the Greek addition to Merneptah because the details fit a later explanatory gloss about Gezer, especially in relation to Solomon's period. He treats it as scribal expansion rather than independent evidence for the conquest chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=12377" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:26:17</a> | How should political voting be understood biblically or morally, especially with 1 Corinthians 5:1-5? | He says voting is a civic duty and 1 Corinthians 5 addresses church discipline, so voters must weigh candidates by conscience and context. | He separates the church-discipline situation in 1 Corinthians 5 from modern electoral choices. Voting belongs to civic responsibility, so Christians should weigh available candidates, consequences, and conscience rather than apply that passage as if it directly settles elections. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=12932" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:35:32</a> | Were there political philosophy texts in ancient Egypt meant to educate and guide rulers? | He says none are known; rulers were trained in royal settings, and Egypt was not a political-philosophy culture in that sense. | He says Egypt had wisdom literature and royal instruction, but not political philosophy texts like later Greek theory. Kings learned in palace and elite contexts, yet the culture did not produce the same kind of abstract constitutional or political treatises. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=12990" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:30</a> | What ancient texts are you sad we lost? | He names the original Book of Jashar and Egyptian court daybooks, especially for important reigns such as the Hyksos period. | He says he would especially like texts that could illuminate biblical and Egyptian historical questions: the real Book of Jashar and Egyptian court records or daybooks. Such documents would help reconstruct events and reigns that are now poorly documented. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=13328" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:08</a> | What is the best argument that Jewish traditions borrowed from Zoroastrianism in exile, and why is it wrong? | He says he is not qualified to answer because Zoroastrianism and Persian-period Jewish literature are outside his specialization. | He declines to answer because the question requires expertise in Zoroastrianism and Persian-period Judaism that he does not claim. Rather than inventing an answer, he marks the limits of his competence and leaves the question to specialists. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=13430" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:50</a> | Is Leah a foil to a bovine goddess? | He initially does not understand the question; after clarification, he says the weak-eyed/Hathor connection is too oblique to support the claim. | After working out what the question means, he finds the proposed connection too indirect. The association between Leah's eyes and a bovine or Hathor-like motif is not strong enough for him to treat Leah as a deliberate foil to such a goddess. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=13540" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:40</a> | What should a scholar do if they repeatedly get the scholarship on an issue wrong? | He says scholars should do due diligence, admit mistakes, learn, and correct themselves rather than be automatically excluded from discussion. | He says errors should be corrected honestly, especially when someone has failed to do enough research. Repeated mistakes damage credibility, but the proper response is due diligence, public correction, and learning rather than automatic permanent disqualification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=13844" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:44</a> | What is your opinion of the latest Shroud evidence and new dating information? | He says not to pin hopes on it, notes serious reservations, and says a fuller video is planned. | He urges caution and says the new claims should not become the basis for anyone's faith. He has reservations about the evidence and intends a fuller treatment, so the live-stream answer stays deliberately tentative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=13940" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:20</a> | Did you see Dan's response to Michael Jones? | He says yes, and also saw Michael Jones's response, but gives only a brief critical reaction. | He confirms that he saw both sides of the exchange but does not launch into a full analysis. His brief reaction indicates disagreement or criticism without turning the answer into a detailed review of the debate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=14090" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:50</a> | Was Abraham Syrian? | He says Abraham was northern Mesopotamian and Amorite, not Syrian, though Amorites originated in the northern Levant. | He distinguishes later Syrian identity from Abraham's older ethnic and geographic setting. Abraham is best described as from northern Mesopotamia and connected with Amorite background, even if Amorite origins overlap broadly with the northern Levant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=14224" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:04</a> | If an ancient Mesopotamian mentioned god without specificity, might they be invoking a vague all-powerful deity? | He says it could depend on Mesopotamian context, but Egypt provides a counterexample where an unspecified god often means a local or highest deity. | He says the answer depends on the specific culture, text, and context, so he does not generalize too quickly about Mesopotamia. He compares Egypt, where an unspecified god can refer to a local deity or highest relevant deity rather than a vague abstract monotheistic being. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/80oRlFB_V60?t=14371" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:31</a> | What do you think of historiography, or history through narrative? | He distinguishes that from a narrative fallacy and says narrative forms can still do real historical work. | He says telling history narratively is not the same as committing a narrative fallacy. Historical writing necessarily selects, orders, and explains events, and narrative form can be legitimate if it remains disciplined by evidence. |
